title-group.article-title: Rapid Mechanochemical Synthesis of Oxyhalide Superionic Conductor: Time‐Resolved Structural Evolution

aff.named-content: Department of Physics and Institute of Major Scientific Facilities for New Materials
aff.institution: Southern University of Science and Technology
Japan Synchrotron Radiation Research Institute SPring‐8

aff.named-content: Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Energy Materials for Electric Power
aff.institution: Southern University of Science and Technology

aff.named-content: Shenzhen Key Laboratory of Natural Gas Hydrate

permissions.copyright-statement: © 2025 The Author(s). Small Methods published by Wiley‐VCH GmbH

license.license-p: This is an open access article under the terms of the http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/4.0/ License, which permits use, distribution and reproduction in any medium, provided the original work is properly cited and is not used for commercial purposes.

abstract.title: Abstract
abstract.p: The design and synthesis of advanced solid electrolytes (SEs) underlie the development of safety and high‐energy density all‐solid‐state batteries (ASSBs). Mechanochemical synthesis stands as the predominant method, yet it faces criticism due to its energy and time‐intensive process (typically spanning several hours to days), presenting a significant obstacle to large‐scale industrial production. Furthermore, ambiguity surrounding the formation mechanisms of SEs during mechanochemical reactions has limited optimization efforts. In addressing these challenges, evidence is presented that the efficiency of mechanochemical SE synthesis can achieve remarkable heights through process optimization. Specifically, the rapid synthesis of the state‐of‐the‐art Li‐Nb‐O‐Cl superionic conductor in only a few hours is highlighted, while concurrently demonstrating its superior electrochemical performance. Notably, for the first time, a structural evaluation during the mechanochemical reaction by time‐resolved in situ synchrotron X‐ray scattering experiments unveils a two‐stage process. This expeditious mechanochemical synthesis of SEs establishes a foundational step toward the commercialization of ASSBs.

Time‐resolved synchrotron X‐ray scattering coupled with mechanochemical synthesis reveals the structural evolution of oxyhalide superionic conductors. Oxygen incorporation governs reaction kinetics, amorphization, and microstructural transformations, while enabling efficient Li‐ion diffusion. This study highlights critical correlations between reaction mechanisms and structure, informing the rational design of advanced solid electrolytes.

institution-wrap.institution: National Natural Science Foundation of China
Zhejiang Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China
Shenzhen Key Laboratory of Solid State Batteries
Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Energy Materials for Electric Power
Guangdong‐Hong Kong‐Macao Joint Laboratory for Photonic‐Thermal‐Electrical Energy Materials and Devices
Key Laboratory of Energy Conversion and Storage Technologies
Natural Science Foundation of Zhejiang Province
Natural Science Foundation of Ningbo Municipality

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: The extended milling duration not only incurs high time costs but also exacerbates the depreciation of machinery, rendering this method unideal for large‐scale production of SEs. The effective design of halide SEs with certain distortion and amorphization can greatly enhance the ionic conductivity. However, the actual synthesis of these materials remains difficult when using straightforward synthesis methods.

Here, we show compelling evidence that mechanochemical synthesis of SEs holds significant promise for achieving desirable high efficiency through optimized processing techniques. As a case study, the state‐of‐the‐art Li‐Nb‐O‐Cl (LNOC) electrolyte, which exhibits a record high ionic conductivity for halide SEs (>10 mS cm−1), is prepared by a mere few hours of mechanochemical reaction, resulting in a marked reduction in processing time. After only 2 h of reaction, the product maintains a superionic conductivity exceeding 1 mS cm−1. Remarkably, we performed time‐resolved in situ synchrotron X‐ray scattering (XRS) with home‐built ball milling devices, for the first time, which illuminated the mechanochemical formation pathways of SEs in response to varying precursors, and identified an interesting two‐stage process in the optimized rapid reaction. The LNOC solid electrolyte demonstrates commendable electrochemical performance across a broad temperature range in all‐solid‐state batteries. These novel findings are expected to provide valuable guidance for the development and synthesis of next‐generation solid electrolyte materials.

sec.title: Results and Discussion

caption.p: Li+ ionic transport of LNOC solid electrolyte. a) Li+ ionic conductivity of xLiOH:NbCl5 SEs (x = 0.75–1.05). b) Arrhenius plots and the calculated activation energy for LNOC (x = 0.9) and LiNbCl6 at a wide temperature range from −40 to 80 °C. c) The summary of Arrhenius plots of the LNOC SE and representative Li‐ion solid electrolytes reported so far. d) The ionic conductivity evolution of LNOC SE depends on ball milling time compared to halide SEs synthesized via the ball milling method.

sec.p: The superionic LNOC SE, subjected to 24 h of ball milling time, exhibits an average particle size of 6.5 µm, with the largest particles, as illustrated in Figure S4 (Supporting Information). In addition, energy‐dispersive X‐ray spectroscopy mapping of the LNOC powder reveals a homogeneous distribution of Nb, O, and Cl throughout the sample (Figure S5, Supporting Information). To further elucidate the influence of mechanochemical treatment on the microstructure evolution, a systematic analysis was conducted using scanning electron microscopy (SEM) on LNOC samples that were subjected to ball milling for different durations (Figures S6–S12, Supporting Information). During the first 3 h, SEM images show the coexistence of large NbCl5 precursor particles alongside smaller particles indicative of the forming solid electrolyte phase. After a duration of 10 to 15 h of ball milling, the conglomeration of particles is observable, and previously smooth, large precursor particles are no longer present. Long‐term ball‐milling synthesis over 24 h results in a further narrowing of the particle size distribution with the formation of a larger number of smaller particles, accompanied by minor agglomeration of these small particles into larger clusters. Notably, the ionic conductivity increases rapidly in the early stages of milling, ultimately reaching a plateau of about 10 mS cm−1 after 24 h, indicating that a structurally and morphologically optimized state has been reached (Figure S13, Supporting Information). These findings underscore the critical role of controlled particle size and morphology in enhancing ion transport in chloride‐based solid electrolytes, thereby offering valuable insights for the rational design of high‐performance superionic conductors.

caption.p: The structure determination and cryo‐TEM characterization of LNOC SE. a) Rietveld refinement of synchrotron X‐ray diffraction (wavelength = 0.68867 Å). b, c) Rietveld refinements of TOF neutron diffraction data for LNOC. b) Low‐q region with structural coherence up to Nb‐Nb distances (≈6.3 Å). c) High‐q region showing refined local coordination around Nb (Nb‐O/Cl, ≈1.97 Å). d) Crystal structures based on the Rietveld refinement of the bc plane and the ac plane. e) Cryo‐TEM overview image of LNOC particle. Cryo‐HRTEM images of the selected regions (insert shows a lattice parameter). SAED patterns of the selected regions with indexing (marked by green and blue squares).

sec.p: However, the current high‐symmetry structural model may not allow us to distinguish between the two anions based on positional refinement alone. Therefore, we have accepted a reasonable level of misfit in both the X‐ray and neutron diffraction refinements and instead focused on refining the occupancy fraction of Li+. After careful refinement, we found that the Li+ ion occupies a position that enables the formation of a stable network with Cl and O atoms, bridging two neighboring octahedral motifs (Figure 2d). This supports the structural stability of the material, even after undergoing high‐energy ball milling.

To further understand the degree of distortion associated with oxygen in the structure, we performed local structure modelling based on pair distribution function analysis (PDF) using the neutron total scattering pattern. As reported structure in the referenced study using Cc structure, oxygen exhibits a highly asymmetric coordination within the octahedron, and the O─Nb─O linkage is significantly distorted from linearity. Two distinct Nb─O bond lengths of 1.75 and 2.50 Å were identified, where the values are relatively rare and considered unstable for Nb─O bonds in naturally occurring structures. Moreover, the reported Cc structure from PDF analysis could not simultaneously satisfy both reciprocal space (average structure) and real‐space (local structure) modelling, suggesting that the proposed structural model may require further investigation.

In this work, to more reasonably interpret the structure of LixNbOxCl5‐x, we aimed to model both reciprocal and real space using a unified structural model. Specifically, we used the high‐symmetry structure obtained from reciprocal space (XRD) refinement as the basis for real‐space modelling, adjusting only a few parameters directly related to oxygen distortion—namely the β angle and the X/Y/Z coordinates of the oxygen atoms—while strictly maintaining the I4/m symmetry. From the results, only minor shifts in the oxygen positions were observed, and the simulated pair distribution function showed excellent agreement with the experimental data (Figure S19, Supporting Information). The β angle was determined to be 90.0568°, and the fractional coordinates of the O/Cl site were (0, −0.0207(3), 0.5738(1). The position of Nb is distorted (0.0107(1),0.0004(2), 0). These minimal distortions suggest that the high‐symmetry I4/m model remains largely preserved in real space. The excellent agreement between the simulated and experimental pair distribution functions further supports this conclusion, indicating that the previously reported 1.75 Å bond length may result from termination ripples or possibly minor impurity phases rather than intrinsic structural features of the LixNbOxCl5‐x.

In this I4/m‐type structure, the Nb atoms are six‐fold coordinated by two axial O2− and four equatorial Cl− ions, forming slightly distorted NbO2Cl4 octahedra that align into infinite chains along the c‐axis. These chains are separated by disordered Li+ ions that occupy interstitial sites between the octahedra, allowing Li ions to pass freely during the charging and discharging process. In contrast, the crystal structure of Nb‐based SE without O‐doping, as LiNbCl6, demonstrates a trigonal phase in the R3 space group (Figure S20, Supporting Information).

Cryogenic high‐resolution transmission electron microscopy (cryo‐HRTEM) and selected area electron diffraction (SAED) were employed to investigate the microstructure of the oxyhalide material in order to reduce the irradiation from the electron beam (Figure 2e). As can be seen from the cryo‐TEM overview image, the LNOC particle shows an irregular shape without noticeable crystal faces. Cryo‐HRTEM image of the selected region (green frame) shows the presence of crystalline nanoparticles (highlighted in red circles), as well as the presence of an amorphous component, such as uneven surface boundaries and structural distortions. The elemental mapping indicates homogeneous incorporation of Nb, O, and Cl into the amorphous and crystalline matrix (Figure S21, Supporting Information). To further elucidate the structural characteristics of the LNOC solid electrolyte, two representative zones were examined by selected area electron diffraction (SAED). In the first region, clear lattice fringes with an interplanar spacing of 0.397 nm were observed. The corresponding pattern exhibits well‐defined diffraction spots, confirming the crystalline nature of this domain, as indicated by the selected region (green frame). In contrast, the second region (blue frame) displayed no discernible lattice contrast in the HRTEM image and demonstrated solely diffuse halo rings in the SAED pattern, indicating a predominantly amorphous phase. Although the coexistence of crystalline and amorphous regions is evident in microscopy, the broadened peaks observed in the X‐ray diffraction pattern are most likely due to the limited crystallite size and microstrains within the crystalline domains, a common consequence of high‐energy ball milling processes. Collectively, these structural data indicate that the synthesized LNOC material exhibits both partial amorphization and nanocrystallinity. The key factor contributing to the elevated conductivity of LNOC is the presence of the nanocrystalline phase embedded within the amorphous matrix. This specific configuration facilitates the transfer of lithium ions between the crystalline nanoparticles of the SE and ensures grain‐boundary‐free ion transport. Cryo‐TEM studies have permitted the identification of the amorphous matrix and its interaction with crystalline nanoparticles. However, to gain a comprehensive understanding of the ion transport mechanism and to optimize conductivity, it is imperative to focus on the crystalline structure.

The ex situ XRD analysis depends on ball‐milling time for LNOC SE was carried out to understand the formation of oxychloride structure (see Figure S22, Supporting Information), but certain limitations hindered the capture of subtle phase transitions during the milling process, allowing for the identification of stable phases only after milling is complete. Consequently, to effectively monitor the formation pathway of both oxychloride and chloride SEs, we performed synchrotron X‐ray scattering (XRS) techniques during high‐frequency ball milling, as presented in Figure
3a,b. It should be mentioned that in planetary mills, the energy transfer predominantly occurs through shear forces, whereas in vibrator mills, the direct impact of the balls is more significant. Given that it is not feasible to ascertain the quantity and positions of lithium using X‐ray techniques, a viable alternative for qualitatively assessing the rate of mechanochemical reactions is to monitor the change in the profiles of Bragg peaks for NbCl5 during conversion to the final product. For both LNOC and LNC SEs experimental setup, the final product was identified after 180 min at a frequency of 25 Hz. For both experiments, LNOC and LNC SEs present well‐defined characteristic Bragg peaks in the range of 0.8°–2.7° (Figure 3c,d) under 114.29 KeV synchrotron X‐ray. Therefore, this methodology permits the estimation of the qualitative rate of the oxidation or chlorination reaction can be estimated using this approach. In both scenarios, the first reflections are the same and belong to the NbCl5 structure, while the additional reflections at 1.46° and 1.69° arise from the utilized steel balls.

caption.p: Study on the synthesis mechanism of SEs. a,b) Scheme of study on the synthesis mechanism of Nb‐based SEs. c,d) in situ monitoring of milling for 180 min of c) 0.9LiOH:NbCl5 and d) LiCl:NbCl5. e) Phase transformation from initial precursors to oxyhalide structure.

sec.p: Figure 3c shows the time‐dependent diffractions during mechanochemical synthesis up to 180 min, which starts from LiOH and NbCl5 in a molar ratio of 0.9:1. Real‐time monitoring patterns show dimensional destruction of the structure during the mechanochemical reaction. The primary reflections shown at the beginning of the reaction were well identified with the NbCl5 structure. During the first 13 min of grinding (Figure 3c; Figure S23a,b, Supporting Information), the intensity of Bragg peaks was greatly related to the NbCl5 structure. This could lead to the dimensional destruction of the NbCl5 structure along (310) and (042) orientations, owing to substantial mechanical impact. As a result, only a singular unit of NbCl5 was preserved within the evolving structure. In the subsequent stage of the crystallization process, isolated NbCl5 units incorporate oxygen to form NbO2Cl4 polyhedra, which undergo linear and 2D aggregation, progressively bridging along the (110) and (200) crystallographic planes over the next 10 min of mechanochemical activation. This directional assembly marks the transition from a disordered state to an emerging framework via the (101), where polyhedral units begin to align and connect coherently within the developing I4/m symmetry. Collectively, the reflections at 0.97°, 2.10°, and 2.43° are unambiguously attributed to the (110), (200), and (101) planes of the I4/m phase, highlighting the progressive assembly of a 3D network (Figure 3c,e; Figure S23, Supporting Information). It is noteworthy that the crystalline LNOC structure formed through this pathway remains stable throughout extended milling durations, lasting up to 180 min.

In contrast, the chlorination rate of the LiCl and NbCl5 reaction is significantly lower than the oxidation reaction. This results in the destruction of the (310) and (040) planes within the NbCl5 structure, subsequently leading to the gradual formation of a new layer along the (001) plane (Figure S23c,d, Supporting Information). Upon completion of the reactions, a mixture of the starting materials, NbCl5 and newly synthesized LiNbCl6, was obtained as indicated by the reflections at (042) and (003), respectively (Figure 3d). The intensity of the reflection increased during the final 10 min, culminating in maximum values, which is evidenced by the appearance of a red tint on the (003) plane. An increase in the intensity of the LNC SE suggests that crystallization of the sample occurred during the milling process, leading to the formation of a close‐packing sublattice, which may limit the ionic conductivity of the material. As a result, a sandwich‐like quasi‐two‐dimensional LiNbCl6 structure completely devoid of oxygen is synthesized, as shown in Figure S20b (Supporting Information).

Migration paths and associated energy barriers were calculated using the valence bond center energy (BVSE) method.[
43 , 44 , 45 , 46
] The obtained results are in good agreement with the experimental data, elucidating the reason underlying the phenomenon of rapid ionic conductivity. As shown in Figure
4a, the disordered main structure presents multiple sites for Li ions movement along the c direction, and the activation energy is relatively negligible, only 0.057 eV. In the macroscopic crystal, these isolated 1D channels are actually always blocked by some defects in the channels. For LNOC SE, a rotating NbO2Cl4 group might be a possible low‐energy defect that causes this blocking. Therefore, the activation energy of 0.25 eV observed experimentally near room temperature is very close to the predicted migration energy of 0.225 eV for the 3D migration path network involving all Li sites. This network is formed by the sites (i1 and i2) along the c direction through the two equivalent vertical directions with Li and i3 sites (see Figure 4b). By comparison, the Li‐ion diffusivity in LiNbCl6 SE exhibits a high energy barrier over 0.5 eV, which was evaluated using the BVSE calculation (Figure 4c,d). The high energy barrier is related to the close packing of LiCl6 and NbCl6 into the trigonal structure R3 space group, which severely limits the mobility of Li ions in Oct‐Tet‐Oct diffusion channels. For this reason, mixed anion chemistry, where a metal cation is coordinated by more than one type of anionic ligand to form a heteroleptic polyhedron, can lead to significant structural modifications. These changes can lead to increased ionic conductivity that is not achievable in compounds containing only one type of anion.

caption.p: Theoretical analysis of the Li transport behaviour in Li0.9NbO0.9Cl4.1 and LiNbCl6 SEs. a,c) BVSE models for migration energy barriers of a) Li0.9NbO0.9Cl4.1 and c) LiNbCl6 SE; b, d) BVSE maps showing 2D migration paths for b) Li0.9NbO0.9Cl4.1 SE and d) LiNbCl6 viewed as a blue constant EBVSE (Li) iso‐surface superimposed on a crystal structure.

sec.p:
The superionic LNOC material has been tested as a solid‐state electrolyte in ASSBs at 30 °C. The electrochemical performance of ASSBs at the voltage range of 3.32–4.42 V versus Li+/Li is presented in Figure
5a–d. LiNi0.8Co0.1Mn0.1O2 (scNCM811) powder was used as a cathode material, and Li‐In alloy was used as an anode. A thin layer of sulfide electrolyte LPSC was placed to prevent the LNOC and Li‐In alloy reaction. The rate capability at various current densities of assembled ASSBs is shown in Figure 5a,b. While the speed was gradually increased step‐by‐step from 0.2C to 1C, the magnitude of the capacitance drop was negligible. The values of the average capacities for 0.2C, 0.33C, 0.5C, 0.8C, 1C, and 2C were 159.9, 152.1, 142.5, 131.4, 125.5, and 105.8 mAh g−1, respectively, which are reasonable given the use of a solid‐state configuration.[
50
]

caption.p: Electrochemical performance of Ni‐rich ASSBs with LNOC SE. a) Rate capability at 0.2C–2C at RT. b) Charge/discharge curves at different current densities at RT. c) Long‐term cycling performance at 0.5C at RT. d) Long‐term cycling performance at 2C at RT. e) Rate capability at 0.03C–0.5C at −18 °C. f) Charge/discharge curves at different current densities at −18 °C. g) Long‐term cycling performance at −18 °C. h) Rate capability at 0.2C–2C at −30 °C. i) Charge/discharge curves at different current densities at −30 °C.g) Long‐term cycling performance at 0.1C at 30 °C. k) Long‐term cycling performance at 0.2C at −30 °C.

sec.p: Additionally, cross‐sectional SEM images and corresponding EDS elemental mapping analysis on different interfacial areas were conducted for a comprehensive understanding of the electrochemical stability after 100 cycles. The interfacial area of LNOC SE|scNCM811 cathode particles remains extremely dense and tight even after charge/discharge cycles, resulting in an undamaged cathode layer. On the other hand, the interfacial area of LNOC SE in Figure S29 (Supporting Information) showed an undamaged cathode layer with well‐particle‐particle morphology of the composite on the cathode electrode, which confirms electrochemical stability and maintains the geometric continuity after cycling. Thus, the introduction of oxygen increases the average bond strength in the anionic framework and reduces the electronic polarizability of the electrolyte. This makes the LNOC matrix less susceptible to oxidative degradation when used with high‐voltage cathodes. In particular, the mixed coordination of anions suppresses the formation of easily oxidizable Cl‐rich compounds, thereby expanding the oxidative stability window.

More importantly, the ionic conductivity of LNOC shows the applicable value at low temperatures, comparable with sulfide and liquid electrolytes. The ASSBs at low temperatures were also tested (Figure 5e–k). Before the ASSB with LNOC SE test, the electrochemical window was measured by cyclic voltammetry measurements using Li‐In/LPSC/LNOC/LNOC+CNT cells at −18 °C, and the results are presented in Figure S30 (Supporting Information). The electrochemical studies have shown that the electrochemical stability window has become wider, resulting in a potential window from 3.12–4.42 V versus Li+/Li. Figure 5e shows the results of electrochemical charge/discharge tests at various current densities for ASSBs with LNOC SE. During electrochemical tests at low temperatures, the specific capacity of the battery at a current of 0.1C decreased significantly, which could be due to the poor electronic conductivity of the cathode material. Therefore, 2% CNT was added to the oxide cathode material to increase the electronic conductivity, which greatly improved the charge/discharge performance of ASSB at −18 °C, as presented in Figure S31a (Supporting Information). At a low rate of 0.03C, the reversible capacity was 158 mAh g−1, and at higher rates of 0.1C, 0.33C, and 0.5C, the reversible capacitances remained at 140.3, 113.5, and 101.4 mAh g−1, respectively (Figure 5e,f). Even when the current densities were increased to 0.8 and 1C, effective Li (de)integration processes still took place, as evidenced by the charge/discharge curves in Figure 5f. It is worth noting that the charge/discharge curves showed smooth redox plateaus at low temperatures, which shows the efficient operation of a solid battery at low temperatures. Figure 5g demonstrates that the overall capacity reduction at the long‐term stability cycling at 1C current density at low temperature for ASSB with LNOC SE is 70.6% after 400 cycles with a high average Coulomb efficiency of 99.8%.

In addition, since LNOC SE exhibits ionic conductivity ≥10−4 S cm−1 at −30 °C, we further performed the electrochemical testing of ASSBs at −30 °C. The electrochemical performance results at −30 °C are presented in Figure 5h–k. The additive 4% CNT to the cathode material mixture to raise the electronic conductivity significantly improves the capacitance performance of the battery compared to adding 2% CNT at −30 °C, as shown in Figure S31b (Supporting Information). The discharge capacity of ASSBs at 0.03C, 0.1C, and 0.3C are 138.2, 95.9, and 38.6 mAh g−1, respectively (Figure 5h,i). When the current returns to 0.1C again, the reversible capacity of 95 mAh g−1 can still be reached. Figure 5j shows the cycle performance of ASSB with LNOC SE at a current density of 0.1C after the rate capability test. According to the electrochemical test result, the reversible capacity can still be maintained at 66 mAh g−1 and 70.9% retention with a high average Coulomb efficiency of 99.6% after more than 200 cycles.

In order to further study the long cycle life of ASSBs with LNOC SE at −30 °C, the ultra‐long cycle test was performed at 0.2C for 1000 cycles, which is presented in Figure 5k. It is impressive that the ASSB can still achieve a good initial discharge capacity of 65.9 mAh g−1 at 0.2C at a low temperature. Moreover, electrochemical performance shows only 8.8% and 15.7% capacity decay after 250 and 750 cycles, and maintains a high‐capacity retention of 80.6% with a high average Coulomb efficiency of 99.7% after 1000 cycles. This impressive performance of ASSB with LNOC SE demonstrates durability and functionality at low temperatures, which has previously been very rarely seen with halide‐based SEs.

After increasing the RH to 10%–12%, LNOC SE showed good stability for 12 h with a residual ionic conductivity above 5 × 10−4 S cm−1, while LZC and LIC SEs showed rather low ionic conductivity values (Figure S35, Supporting Information). Notably, LNOC SE maintained conductivities, highlighting promising performance in moisture‐rich environments. In addition, LiNbOCl4 strongly contrasted with LNOC SE by showing a large drop in ionic conductivity to 6.47 × 10−6 S cm−1 (Figure S3 and Table S6, Supporting Information). Due to the presence of hygroscopic LiCl impurities, it can better absorb moisture and thereby promote its penetration into the structure of the solid electrolyte, which leads to low conductivity values. Therefore, the strategy of O‐doping in the structure of halide electrolytes opens the way not only to high ionic conductivity but also to improving moisture stability compared to single‐anion halide materials due to the enhanced bonding energy involving oxygen in oxyhalide SEs. However, this approach is effective only when hygroscopic impurities, such as LiCl, are absent, as such impurities can absorb moisture and compromise the structural integrity and ionic conductivity of the electrolyte.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: In summary, this study employs a combination of advanced experimental techniques for structure characterization in conjunction with theoretical calculations to study the structural features and elucidate the influence of anion mixing on ionic transport in oxychloride solid‐state electrolyte. The structure of the as‐prepared Li0.9NbO0.9Cl4.1 solid electrolyte was determined to crystallize in the I4/m space group, as validated by synchrotron X‐ray and neutron diffraction. High mobility of Li ions drives an impressive conductivity of 1.02 × 10−2 S cm−1 at RT, which is facilitated by the anion mixed structure. The theoretical study shows that the lithium conduction channels adopt a continuous, spiral‐like topology along the c‐axis, forming a 3D, helically interconnected network with a low migration barrier of 0.225 eV. In contrast, LiNbCl6 SE with one anion close‐packing trigonal structure shows an Oct‐Tet‐Oct pathway characterized by a high energy landscape, correlating with a substantially lower ionic conductivity of 4.59 × 10−7 S cm−1 at RT. The results of the formation pathway of halide electrolytes demonstrate a step‐wise transformation, involving amorphization and structural reconstruction before subsequent crystallization, which enhances the understanding of phase transformations and the manifestation of intermediate changes in relation to variations in ionic conductivity. The assembled ASSBs with oxyhalide SE exhibit commendable and stable long‐cycling performance over a wide temperature range. At a high‐rate density of 2 C and 1 C, the capacity retentions are 70.1% and 70.6% after 500 cycles and 400 cycles at room and low temperatures, respectively. Furthermore, a stable performance of 1000 cycles at 0.2C with a capacity retention of 80.6% can be reached in the environment of −30 °C. In addition, the improved moisture stability and cost‐competitiveness of LNOC SE pave the way for their potential commercialization in all‐solid‐state batteries. This study provides valuable insights into crucial synthesis criteria during mechanochemical synthesis and offers guidance for the design of innovative halide superionic conductors. Such advancements are poised to enhance the stability of solid‐state lithium batteries, effectively addressing safety concerns.

sec.title: Material Synthesis of Solid Electrolytes
sec.p: The oxychloride xLiOH:NbCl5 (x = 0.75‐1.05) SEs were synthesized by the ball milling method (BM). All chemicals raw reagents with an analytical grade, such as LiOH (99.9%, Aladdin), and NbCl5 (99% Macklin), were mixed in appropriate ratios using a ball planetary mill (FRITSCH PULVERISETTE 7) at 500 rpm min−1 for 24 h in ZrO2 jar with three φ10 mm, five φ5 mm, and 21 φ3 mm zirconia balls. The mass ratio of powder raw materials to balls is 1:20. All processes are prepared in an Ar‐filled glove box (O2 < 0.01 ppm, H2O < 0.01 ppm).

The resulting chemical equation of the mechanochemical process is represented below:
(1) xLiOH+NbCl5=LixNbOxCl5−x+xHCl↑, where x = 0.75–1.05.
The LiNbCl6 and Li2ZrCl6 materials were synthesized by the same ball milling method. The starting materials were LiCl (99.9%, Aladdin) and NbCl5 (99% Macklin) or ZrCl4 (98% Macklin).

sec.title: Materials Characterization
sec.p: Powder X‐ray diffraction (XRD) measurements of SEs were collected using the Malvern Panalytical Empyrean X‐ray diffractometer employing CuKα radiation (λ = 1.5406 Å) at 40 kV and 45 mA). The samples were placed in the cavity of the zero‐diffraction plate in an Ar‐filled glove box, and the top of the sample holder was covered by a polyamine film to protect against exposure to air.
The morphology and elemental mapping analysis studies of SE materials utilized a high‐resolution field emission scanning electron microscope JEOL Ltd./JSM‐7160FPlus equipped with an EDX detector.

LNOC solid‐state electrolyte was studied by cryo‐TEM on a scanning/transmission electron microscope (S/TEM) operated at 300 kV. Particles were dispersed onto a TEM lacey carbon grid inside an Ar‐filled glovebox. The specimen was then taken out into an air‐tight container and immediately plunged into liquid nitrogen, followed by transferring the specimen onto a pre‐cooled cryo‐TEM holder (Elsa, Gatan, USA) using a cryotransfer station to ensure the entire process occurred under a cryogenic environment, which keeps the specimen in its native state. Then, the cryo‐TEM holder was inserted into the TEM column for characterization at a low temperature (100 K).

The infrared (IR) spectra were obtained using an FTIR spectrometer (Thermo Scientific Nicolet iS50 FTIR Spectrometer) in the range of 400–2000 cm−1, which is placed in the glove box to prevent the measured electrolytes from coming into contact with air.

The FTIR spectra of gas in the ball milling jar were carried out using an FTIR spectrometer (Brucker VERTEX 70v) with ATR‐PROBE IN350‐TEX attachment, which connects by a tube with a ball milling jar after mechanochemical reactions for detecting escaping gas after opening the spool valve on the jar cover.

Raman spectra were measured on a DXR3 Raman Microscope equipped with a 532 nm laser. Powder of solid electrolyte was placed in a glass capillary with a diameter of 1 mm in an Ar‐filled glove box, which was sealed with a LOXEAL UV 30‐23 adhesive to prevent contact with air during measurements.

The synchrotron X‐ray data collected at beamline 11‐ID of APS were used for refinement. A neutron diffraction study was conducted on the Multi‐Physics Instrument (MPI) at the China Spallation Neutron Source.

The 1H solid‐state NMR (ssNMR) experiments were performed on a Bruker Avance NEO 400 spectrometer equipped with a 9.4 T wide‐bore magnet. The 1H MAS NMR spectra were obtained via one pulse sequence with a π/2 pulse length of 2.27 µs and a recycle delay of 2.0 s. All the ssNMR experiments were operated at a spinning rate of 5 kHz at room temperature via a 3.2 mm HXY probe. 1H shifts with respect to adamantane (1.8 ppm).

sec.title: in situ Monitoring of the Synthesis Mechanism
sec.p: The real‐time experiments on the synthesis mechanism of Nb‐based solid electrolytes were conducted using a specially designed milling machine and an in‐house‐designed milling jar from BL08W at Spring‐8, Japan, which is equipped with a PMMA X‐ray window in the middle parts, and enhances the X‐ray signal while minimizing the loss of mechano impact. It also features two layers of thick walls on the sides to facilitate maximum horizontal mechano impact, ensuring efficient grinding and milling. The wavelength used was 0.1078 Å. To track the phase transition of halide materials from the precursors, a collimated beam with 1 × 1 mm2 size and a flat‐panel detector PE1621En was employed.

sec.title: Computational Details
sec.p:
The interface pseudo‐binary reaction energy was calculated as follows:
(2) ΔEDSE,H2O,x=EeqCinterfaceCse,CH2O,x−EinterfaceSE,H2O,x where CSE and CH2O or CLNOC are the compositions of SE and water or LNOC SE. The x is the molar fraction of the SE. The energies of materials were obtained from the Materials Project database.[
52
]

sec.title: Measurements of Ionic and Electronic Conductivities
sec.p:
Prior to the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and direct current (DC) polarization measurements, the powders of solid electrolytes (SEs) were cold‐pressed at 3 tons (260 MPa) in a polyether ether ketone (PEEK) mold with a diameter of 10 mm, and stainless steel (SS) rods were used as blocking electrodes on both sides of the pellets. The DC polarization was carried out using the Autolab PGSTAT 302F at RT. The EIS measurements were measured using a Biologic VSP200 electrochemical station in the measurement frequency range of 0.01–10 MHz with 10 mV driving potential amplitude with respect to the open‐circuit voltage at 30 °C. The Li+ ionic conductivity of LNOC SEs was calculated by the equation:
(3) σ=RSE·St−1 where σ – the ionic conductivity of the solid electrolyte; S – an area of contact between the solid electrolyte and the SS rods; RSE – resistance value of the SE; t – thickness of the SE pellets. The area of contact SSǀSE for all samples is 0.785 cm2.

The activation energy was obtained by EIS measurements in the temperature range from −40 to 80 °C. The Swagelok cell was set in a variable temperature oven (Guangzhou Wusuo Environment Instrument, HSL/G‐04). Every temperature value was held for 1.5 h before collecting data to ensure that the thermal equilibrium was reached. The Ea is calculated by the following Arrhenius equation:
(4) σT=Aexp−EakBT where σ is the tested ionic conductivity, T is the Kelvin temperature, and kB is the Boltzmann constant.

sec.title: All‐Solid‐State Battery (ASSB) Cell Preparation and Battery Performance Measurement
sec.p: The all‐solid‐state Li‐ion batteries were assembled in a mold cell consisting of two stainless steel rods and a 10 mm diameter cylinder made of insulating polycarbonate. First, 85 mg of LNOC powder was filled into a cylinder and cold pressed at 200 MPa for 2 min. Then, 7 mg of scNCM811 cathode was spread on one side of the LNOC tablet and uniaxially pressed at 300 MPa for 3 min. On the other side of the LNOC SE layer, evenly spread 45 mg Li6PS5Cl sulfide solid‐state electrolyte to avoid the reaction of the LNOC electrolyte with the anode, and pressurize 200 MPa for 1 min. Finally, place a thin Indium foil (0.042 g in weight, 100 µm) with a diameter of 9 mm and Li foil (0.006 g in weight, 6 mm in diameter) on the LPSC electrolyte. Then, a constant uniaxial pressure of 200 MPa was applied to the cell using stainless steel frame screws.

sec.title: Humidity Tolerance Test
sec.p: Humidity tolerance tests were performed on 0.25 g of Li0.9NbO0.9Cl4.1, LiNbOCl4, Li2ZrCl6, and Li3InCl6 (MTI Corporation) powders. The samples were placed in a glovebox equipped with a relative humidity (RH) controller (Changshu Tongrun Electronic Co., Ltd) and filled with argon (Ar) gas. The tests were conducted at 5%–7% and 10%–12% RH at RT for durations of 1, 6, and 12 h, respectively.

sec.title: Statistical Analysis
sec.p: To ensure reproducibility, all electrochemical measurements were performed on at least three nominally identical cells for each configuration: SS/SE/SS, Li–In/Li6PS5Cl/LNOC/LNOC+CNT, and Li–In/Li6PS5Cl/LNOC/scNCM811. Data are presented as mean values ± standard deviation (SD). Statistical processing and graphical presentation of data were performed using OriginPro 2021 software.

sec.title: Conflict of Interest
sec.p: The authors declare no conflict of interest.

ack.title: Acknowledgements
ack.p: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China under Grant No. W2433132, 12204219, 12275119, 51732005, the Shenzhen Science and Technology Program under Grant No. KQTD20200820113047086, the Zhejiang Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China under Grant No. LY23B030003, Natural Science Foundation of Ningbo under Grant No. 2023J200, the Shenzhen Key Laboratory of Solid State Batteries (SYSPG20241211173726011), the Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Energy Materials for Electric Power (2018B030322001), the Guangdong‐Hong Kong‐Macao Joint Laboratory for Photonic‐Thermal‐Electrical Energy Materials and Devices (2019B121205001), the Key Laboratory of Energy Conversion and Storage Technologies (Southern University of Science and Technology), the Ministry of Education. The work was also supported by the Laboratory of Electrochemical Energy Storage Technologies, Academy for Advanced Interdisciplinary Studies (SUSTech), the Major Science and Technology Infrastructure Project of Material Genome Big‐science Facilities Platform supported by Municipal Development and Reform Commission of Shenzhen, and the Center for Computational Science and Engineering at Southern University of Science and Technology. The synchrotron radiation experiments were performed at BL08W at SPring‐8 with the approval of the Japan Synchrotron Radiation Research Institute (JASRI). The proposal numbers are 2021B2006, 2022B1508, and 2023A2341.

sec.title: Data Availability Statement
sec.p: The data that support the findings of this study are available in the supplementary material of this article.
